speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four. Could I ask you when you came to Australia, what was the attitude of-- of Australian towards newcomers then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Things were really good here. It's an improvement over everywhere else. We were told to-- to-- when you're here, you-- you do like the Australians do. So we didn't want to be classified as some of the wogs, they used to refer to people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we ate white bread like everybody else. I didn't take any rye bread to school. We had the normal sandwiches like everybody else has ham sandwiches or whatever, peanut butter and things like that. But not-- nothing-- nothing different. We didn't eat any salamis, or any other things that new Australians would eat in those days. So we try to be as Australians as we could be. And it was OK, yes. People treated us well. I-- most of my friends were only friends of friends I had. A majority of my friends were Jewish, of course, at Melbourne High there were lots of Jewish boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- but very few of my friends, until-- with the exception perhaps of one, Alec Mole, who were actually either born in Australia or came before the war. They sort of kept to themselves. They found that we refugees were a bit something suspicious, you know. We-- I couldn't be trusted. We-- we were perhaps a little bit dirty, although we washed every day. But they thought we had maybe still lice in our hair. We've lost the lice. We ate the lice long ago. They didn't realize that we used to eat those things when we got hungry. But-- so we didn't bring them to Australia. We-- we got rid of them long before. But they sort of-- some of they-- afterwards, I realized what it was. We discussed it at times, that they-- they-- they presented-- the established boys, Jewish boys, that they didn't go to Melbourne High. They went to private schools, those days. Scotch and so on. They-- they-- they were not friendly to us. They thought we're not quite as good as them. But when the exam-- when the exams came, we were. We were a little bit better, actually, because most of us topped the years everything else. Study-wise we're-- we're better than they were. We're much more determined than they were. But they didn't know that. They thought we're sort of a bit devious. But we didn't talk about those things, no. With our friends, we didn't talk about the war. We just kept quiet about it. Even with my best friends for 30, 40 years, some of them now, I don't even know where they were. My best friend, I knew. I found out only some years after that he was a-- he died, unfortunately, a few years ago-- he was a sewer boy from the Warsaw ghetto. Survived by-- in the sewers. Had a hard life, very hard life. I thought I had it tough. He had it much tougher than me. Mine was a picnic. Well, sort of, figuratively speaking. Some people-- when I mentioned that once to my son, he says, some picnic. But you know, some-- some had it much worse than that, yes. Yes. At least I had-- a large part of my survival over the war was my own destiny, when you're partisans at least, you know. You can cut someone's throat. You can do something. If you're sitting in a camp or sitting in a sewer you've got no bloody destiny at all. They catch you, and they kill you, and so on and so forth. You're at the mercy of-- at the sea you've got some-- some chance. It's a bit like playing Russian roulette, but if you've got a blank, it's OK. But if you play Russian roulette without any blanks, that's no good.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's bad news. All right, Hannah. I don't give you a chance to ask me questions. I talk too much, don't I? Did you find out what happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. Some-- there's still conflicting ideas that he was killed there, shortly after in the forest, or just completely disappeared from the sight of the-- no. No. Just gone. I have-- on my 60th birthday last year, I decided to commemorate my father's-- my father in the best way I could do. And I'll be happy to share a photograph of that. I made a memorial plaque at the Holocaust Center for him, and that's-- that's only [INAUDIBLE] way or something left. Here, under, is a extract in one the-- one of them-- someone gave me only a few years ago, an extract in one of the-- And it mentioned here, that Rozencwajg-- my father, it does mention-- he practiced in Luboml, where I told you, during the war. He joined the Polish underground and was killed by the Germans in 1943, which is not exactly right. It was '44, because I still saw him. I know that. But mean-- there's just a witness. So this is the only bit, you know, that's left. There's something, you know. He lived, yeah. But we don't know, really. This is just-- the witnesses aren't talking. But I did look for him, yes, for many years. I wrote a lot. Seems like quite stupid to write to Stalin, write to Churchill, and ask him if-- where my father is. You know, it didn't seem such a bad idea during the war. I forgot to mention something interesting, because a lot of interesting things had happened during my stay in the orphanage. I-- in the orphanage, I was tuned in. I told you I was tuned in. Bad-- bad table manners, but tuned in. Not dumb, just tuned in. And I'd become quite a celebrity, inverted commas, at the orphanage, because I actually developed, over a period of years, a-- a regular fan club. Because we used to be visited-- no, no, no. Don't laugh at me. Yes, I was. I wasn't-- you know, I don't compare myself to Michael Jackson, but you know, I-- I-- I had something going. We used to have ladies coming. The [INAUDIBLE] ladies used to come and visit orphanages. And they used to record our testimonies and stories. They wanted to hear about the children, because they were nice healthy ladies, many of them blond. I think they were-- I was told that they weren't all natural blond, but they looked blond to me. All quite, quite, quite well-fed compared to those of us that came out of the-- from the other side of the border. And they used to listen to our stories. But there was an understanding. It was a-- everything was a-- a game in those days. They used to give us chocolate. And I would only tell them enough for a bit more chocolate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the more chocolate, the more I tell. And I had plenty to tell. And I become a businessman, you know. I used to get chocolate. And I used to tell them interesting things, I mean, what happened to me during the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: They found it interesting. To me, it was just normal, because-- but to them it was something quite-- they were-- some of them were quite fascinated to hear all those things that I was telling them. Similar things that I'm telling you now, only then my memory was even fresher than that. And I was then talking as a-- as a boy of 11, 12, 13, which is more fascinating than now. I mean, sometimes I pretend to be that-- you take back-- you go back in time 50 years. But it's not so easy. But then, it was natural. And they would come back, some of them, and some of them would tell their friends. And they used come up and say, Richard-- they were coming to the house. And they were asking, Richard Rozencwajg, because he had such interesting stories to tell them. And I had my own fan club. And I used to get chocolate. So there you are. Life wasn't all that bad in the orphanage. That's when I wasn't getting stitched up in hospital from my fights. I won a few. I mean, I don't know what happened to some of my other ones. Some of them didn't do so well. But, no, no, we-- no, it was OK. There were-- there were good times. You know, there were good times. Yeah, lots of chocolate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, there you are. Now you know. You're talking to a celebrity. Used to get chocolate for telling my stories. So you know you're not the first one. Sorry. I started telling my testimonies back in 1947, for chocolate, of course. Today, I do it for posterity, for record, for the likes of Irving who deny that it happened. I'd like to ask him where he was in 1943, because I know where I was. He's about the same age as I am, isn't he? David Irving? Maybe a bit older, because I know where I was. So. How do you think that the experiences that you went through during the war affected your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think it affected my life in-- in any adverse way. In some-- I mean, adverse. What do you call adverse? I, like so many others, tried to become a higher achiever. I had some remote success.

speaker: interviewer
text: So whatever we do, whether my friends-- my-- my closest friend is a professor of medicine, at-- and he works in the Children's Hospital, Henry Ekert. Professor Henry Ekert, who survived in Poland, in hiding, similar circumstances to us. And well, his-- and I'm just giving an example-- his whole life was based from Melbourne High. He was going to get to the top of his profession, which he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going to become a Grand Master. The day I picked up my first card 30 years ago, I was going to become a Grand Master. I was going to represent Australia. I was going to have room full of trophies, which I have. I mean, I knew that before it happened. I mean, it might not have happened if it wasn't for the war. You know, I was going to be reasonably well off. I-- I-- I detested poverty. I mean, I simply had so committed to get out of poverty. Even when I was studying, I worked at finishing engineering and getting a wage by working for SAC isn't what I wanted. This isn't going to give me the house in Brighton. It isn't going to give me a holiday house in Charim. It isn't going to give me holidays overseas and so on, and have a good life. Isn't going to send my kids to Brighton Grammar and so forth in Melbourne. That wasn't going to give me. So I-- I was going to find a way of doing it. I had to do it with my own wits, and so, OK, I manage. Properties, investment, and so on. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't think it did me harm in that way, but otherwise, yeah. I lost a few years there. I mean, it's sort of, you know, I mean, playing with a feather is not exactly what I would recommend to a eight or nine-year-old kid. I'd rather play with-- with a bicycle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Started on my first bicycle when I was a-- when I was 15, when I got to Australia. I fell off a bicycle when I was 15. I couldn't ride a bicycle. I started learning swimming at St. Kilda Beach when I was 15. A few other things I know. Some things I missed, I suppose. They took away my youth, yeah, sure, but they gave me something else. They gave me the-- [INAUDIBLE] the ability of solve problems. I'm good at solving problems. I won a lot of-- a lot of chess tournaments. Lots of problems in chess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to show the-- your-- do you have medals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've got some of the things. I will-- I'll-- well, this is probably the most recent one. We won the Australian championships just last month. The-- it was held in Melbourne, the seniors championships. And six of us, and I was a member of that team, which makes me very happy, because I'm getting older but still-- still winning a few. This is probably one of my most prized possessions. It was when I was first time a member of the Australian team. It's Biarritz 1992. This is my world championships medal I got. And that's my number plate, Richard Rozen, Australia. I was very proud of wearing that badge, because, you know, this is my country. I wanted to be Australian, and you can't do much more than represent your country in whatever sport you might be doing. I mean, I think this is probably the-- the ultimate of it, you know, coming out of the forest. When I was catching rats, I didn't think that one day I'll be wearing that medal. Richard Rozen, Australia. It's nice. It's nice to be shaking hands with Omar Sharif and the rest of the international bridge players, playing for other countries, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Coming out of the forest seemed like a long way from playing bridge with Omar Sharif, doesn't it? Mr. Zhivago. That's-- no, they're-- they're the good things, yes. I have won lots of things. But that's-- I'm not here to prize myself. You asked me a question, so I just-- I've got to give you a true answer. I think that's great that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have to lie today. But-- so that made me. I'm pushing. You have to go to the top. You have got to achieve. You've got it-- I mean, it's-- it's-- it's a matter of life or death. Fortunately, I've had the good fortune, sometimes. I mean, I had-- I had a-- a rotten run as well, don't forget. I mean, you know, you can't be unlucky all the time. You've got to have some lucky as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what's come my way, it's good. Good. He's-- he's one of my favorite men. I've always looked up to him very nicely. Simon Wiesenthal. That's his latest book my son gave me for Father's Day. I liked his previous work, and-- which is

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, justice. That's what we want. Justice, yes. Never forgive and never forget, that's my motto. Never forgive and never forget. But otherwise, you go along with it. Occasionally I wake up at nights, even now, still chase them. They chase me. Every time I hear an Alsatian dog bark it brings back memories, especially the big dark ones. Big dark ones, big well-fed dark ones. They bother me, Alsatian dogs. When I was in Europe, I had to go to Austria. I know that I wanted to. I had to, because Richard's family is there. I made an exception of Germany, but Austria I went. Three days, Vienna. I had to do that. I had to do that. And it's a funny thing. When I got out of the bus, in-- because we come by bus from-- from the railway, from wherever we were. Flew in, came by bus, and I saw people walking. And-- and when I saw someone speaking German and limping, and they were in-- in the-- in the age that would have been in their 20s during the war, I used to say very loudly-- my wife had to stop me because they're going to arrest you. I used to say, I bet you he went to the Russian front. See, he lost his toes on the Russian front. I used to say it loudly, because every time I saw an older-- an older man limping, I thought he must have lost his toes on the Russian front. That-- that hatred, that I still have for them. I can't blame the new generation, but I'm talking about the ones that are limping.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he lose his toes? What do you think, he got-- got caught in the-- in the mix box or something? He lost them on the Russian front, as far as I'm concerned. And that makes me happy. I haven't lost-- didn't lose mine. I was there too, but that's OK. What message would you have for-- for your children and for the future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've spent a great deal of time talking to students at the Holocaust Center, where I'm a volunteer. And I talk to them. And my message is that I hope that I'm becoming-- I've always been a bit of a cynic-- but now I think I'm beginning to believe it, to believe it as well, that-- that my cynicism isn't-- isn't totally unfounded. I hope that the world would have learned by our experience, our suffering. Six million of us being slaughtered for no reason at all except belong to a different religion, or believing in-- in a different god or something. That the world will show a bit more tolerance towards people, show more tolerance towards each other. All right, they're not killing any Jews now, but they're killing each other in Yugoslavia just recently, and so on, what's happening in Africa, everywhere else. People are still dying. What happened in-- in Vietnam and Cambodia, those killing fields and so. And it's just a continuity. I-- I-- I think no one has learned about anything. It's just a shame. I mean, I'm not saying that we've died in vain. I mean, we've-- our people have died. I mean, a third of our people got slaughtered. But no one's paying attention. It just goes on all the time. I mean, it seems to be-- it's a sham. It's, in a way-- but as I said, being-- being a cynic and a realist, it doesn't hurt as much as if I was a dreamer and-- and-- and hoping that the world will become a paradise.

speaker: interviewer
text: So many people died because-- because we know it's not. And-- but we hope that the younger generation that listen to-- to-- to the things might somehow or other change. But I know in my lifetime, nothing is going to change. It's just-- just one of those episodes. So, big deal. 6 million Jews died. So what. Big deal. 2 million Cambodians died, big deal. 7-- 350,000 innocent people die in Yugoslavia, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: The numbers are meaningless. It's just-- people are just dying and killing each other all the time. That's, I think, the difference between us and the animals, is that animals usually kill others in self-defense or to eat. We kill for the fun of killing.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's-- can be good fun, I suppose. I remember being part of it once. It wasn't-- it wasn't such a bad game after all, if you survive. But you know. That's my message. Maybe-- maybe somehow, somehow one day the killing will stop. What do you think? It'll stop? I hope so.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can always hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my pleasure. Well, this is one of the very few photographs of my father I have. And on the right is my father, who was just finishing his medical school in Paris, with some of the friends. I understand two of them perished in Poland, went back with him, and one went to America and survived. But I don't know which one. But they were just finishing the medical course, and that's going back to 1931, '32.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was kept for us by family in France. His brother in France who survived. All the photographs which we have here were given to us by the families that survived in France and a few of them were taken to Russia by my auntie, because we had no photographs left. So that's 1931, '32 in Paris, my father. Now this is the-- the earliest photograph that I have of myself, which was sent to Paris. And it survived in Paris. And I'm here in 1938 with my mother. I was three years old here, with my mother at a holiday resort in Poland not far from Warsaw. This is the last and only photographs of my-- well, this is one of the last photographs of my father. And that was in his room in the house we lived in Radom, and that was taken right about '36, '37. Now, that photograph was taken in 1938, about a year before the war. And in that photograph, of course, I am. Right next to me is my auntie Dora. That was my youngest auntie, the one that went to Poland-- from Poland to Russia, my father married. And-- and came back and got us back to Australia. My mother is on the left. My father is next to her, and Dr. And Mrs. Kasdan, my father's best friends, are on the right side with the dog.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this photograph was taken in 1939, early 1939. I was then just over four, four and a bit, and that's a hand-knitted suit I had. I was wearing, as you can see, with my initial, RR. And I'm saluting, because I wanted to become a captain. I wanted to be captain. I couldn't pronounce my name, but Dr. Kasdan told me some 10 years ago that I used to call myself Rozensguy. Captain Rozensguy. I used to be Captain Rozensguy, because I couldn't say Rozencwajg. So I used to say Captain Rozensguy. And I-- and I used to salute. And I still remember that. They were the good days. That photo was also taken in 1939, already. And I would say about six months before the war, 19-- early 1939. Summer in the park, and I'm smiling away, looking quite fat. I lost some of that fat-- fat white-- fat-- fat cheeks as-- as years went by. I started off fat, finished up thin. This was the last photograph before the war of mine and my auntie Dorca, and we're also somewhere in the gardens. And that would be very, very close to the beginning of the war, I would say. Right about May, June already. It's-- it's end of winter, as you can see. And we're getting towards-- towards springs. The last spring before the-- before the war. This is the last photograph of my grandmother from my mother's side, Blicher. Hannah Blicher. She was taken-- they were taken to-- I understand they were taken to Auschwitz from Radom Ghetto. This was a photo in the ghetto. You can see the sad eyes. She looked much-- she was much happier than that when I saw her before that, a year or two before this photo was taken. That photo was taken 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo my auntie-- that's her mother-- Dora, took with her to Russia. That was my maternal grandfather, Haim Blicher. And he also perished in-- together with my grandmother in Treblinka, 1942 or '43, whenever it was. But that photo was taken in 1941. That's the last photo, and already from the Radom Ghetto, and taken out by my auntie, who escaped to Russia. Now, this photo was taken on my arrival to-- in 1946-- in Stuttgart displaced people camp. They were the children of the [INAUDIBLE] school. And oddly enough, five of them came to Australia. And I'm standing next to the teacher. That's the one that's got the ears sticking out and short hair for a obvious reason. And on that photo there's another girl here that-- that lives in Australia, and there's a boy on-- in the back up there, that-- David Waters, who lives in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it on the top row?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, top row, just behind. David Waters. David Wasserstrom, his name was, but now David Waters. He owned the Waters Sunbathing Costume, the largest manufacturer in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are on the top, top row?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, right next to the school teacher. Doesn't that look like me? I was slimmer then.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now here is my-- a photo taken in France already, when I was in the orphanage, with some of the children. That photo was taken in 1947. I'm third, on top row, third on the right. I was here already 12-- 11 years old-- 12 years old. '47. That photo was taken in 1948, and I'm standing just behind the choirmaster. And I was singing in the choir, on the extreme right. That's another photo from the orphanage, and I'm on the extreme right. And it was taken also in '48, '49, '48. That was-- that photo was taken in Paris in a park. And it's [FRENCH], it's called. Famous woods. When my mother and I were sitting on a bench, and that was taken shortly before my departure from-- from France. And that was in 1950, and I was-- I was already here 14, nearly 15 years old. It was in one of my rare occasions that I used to go to Paris. Well, one of the occasions that I used to go to Paris and spend some time with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a photo-- my first photo at school, Brighton State school, in 1951, when I was-- where I was learning English. I'm on the top row, third from the right, smiling away and getting fatter. That photo was taken last year in 1995, and it's to commemorate my father. I had organized a plaque at the Melbourne Holocaust Center, and you can see behind us is Dr. Pinkus Rozencwajg. That's his only memorial, and the only place where he is recognized to have lived and died. I'm here with my mother, who is 92 years old, and she's in a nursing home. She never remarried. That photo was taken in '94. And on this photo we have my son Peter on the right, myself in the rear, our son Mark on the left, and my wife Pescha. That was taken in this house, in Brighton. That photo was taken last year, in '95 where both Governor Richard McGarvie and myself were guest speaker at an opening of Afar. And I thought it was nice. And so all the way from the forest in Poland to having a little chat with the Governor General for Victoria. A long way from the forest. This photograph was taken in 1968 when our late daughter was 15 years old. She died when she was 19. Her name was Sue. Well, it gives me pleasure to introduce my-- my wife, Pescha, who's had to put up with me for now 26 or 27 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel sorry for you. How did you manage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we've had ups and downs. I'm also a child survivor. I was born in Siberia, in Russia, in 1941. So only my mother can testify as to the difficulties. And I was born into a lot of cold climate and I was deprived of the first years of normal babyhood, but I was able to overcome it. We lived in Poland, and so on, and then we came to Australia. Living with a survivor like Richard sometimes is not easy, because he lives through his experiences. And we've always made the best of it, because I do sympathize. I can't say I really understand, because it's nothing that can-- that one can understand unless you go through it. If you go through a divorce, you understand what divorce is about. I've lost a daughter, so I know what it means to lose someone. But to live through what Richard lived through is just very hard to accept. I think he's-- he has made the best of things, and he's come out on top. And he has contributed a great deal to the community, especially to the Holocaust Center, and also in the life-- in the-- the bridge world. And I'm sure he told you that, but-- and I admire that very much. And I tried to help him and support him. Our children were not as sympathetic, but then they just can't understand the concept of it. And as they grow older and they're adults now, they-- they seem to ask more questions and they are more understanding. And we just try to live normal lives, if there is such a thing. But I think it's great that Richard has take it upon himself to-- to tell the story and to share it with others. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think Pescha forgot to mention something about herself. Your involvement in community also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I don't know if you want to know that. We-- we both seem to work for the community. We both-- it just happened that way. We never asked before we got married, what are your interests, what are your dreams? Or what would you like to do in life? And it just so happens that 10 years ago, I gave up my career in real estate and I've decided to change directions. And I got involved in the Jewish community work. And I've been very honored, in July to have become a president of the National Council of Jewish Women of Victoria. And I try to balance my life in community life, as well as I still run a private business of body corporate, and Richard helps me a lot. And since we've got two presidents in one house, it sometimes is quite funny, or quite difficult because he-- we share and compare our problems. And sometimes he says, well, I don't know, you always tell me how terrific your meetings are and I always have problems. And, well, he tends to be much more argumentative, I think, whereas I try to make peace with people and get the best in people. We are different, but we-- we do learn from each other. And-- and I think that's good, because once your children grow up, a lot people grow apart. And I think we enjoy the-- the common interest, which is-- which is really working for other people, which is-- which is just very unusual. If you told me that 20 years ago this is what I would be doing, I wouldn't believe it. I didn't know that I had that capacity or that interest. But I think this is what-- that can grow, and-- and I don't think people know that they've got-- they may have that interest until they-- they try it. Until they give it a chance. That's why I-- I-- I encourage, especially women, to give that a go. And how much-- how fulfilling it can be, how good it is to work for-- for the good of women, Jewish or non-Jewish, and for-- for different projects and different-- just-- just-- just wonderful to-- to expand. Last night we had a speaker, [INAUDIBLE], who was talking about the Queen Vic Women's Hospital. And it's just marvelous that we can-- we all have similar interests and one goal is to improve the state of women around the world. And I think that's-- that's really-- that's really good. And I-- I feel good about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we're lucky. We're lucky that at home, we've got something like 18 chairs, so with two chairmans around here, we-- we don't have to fight for the-- for a chair. I must say that my wife has been very supportive of me. She did say I was-- it's an understatement to say that I am-- can be controversial, but then life sometimes can be difficult. But Pescha has been very supportive of me, had to put up with my moods wins and losses.

speaker: interviewee
text: Confesions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wins and losses. Wins and losses. And-- and also some fails. As long as we win a few more than we lose, it's OK. Yes. It's OK. 27 years. People get less-- less than that for murder. So there you are. She's put--

speaker: interviewee
text: Could be worse.

speaker: interviewee
text: --she's put up with me for that long.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
